subUnits.title: 3.1 Kingdom Fungi, Plantae and Animalia
notes: • Mostly multicellular (except yeasts which are unicellular)
• Heterotrophic by absorption (secrete enzymes externally, absorb nutrients)
• Cell walls made of chitin (not cellulose like plants)
• Store food as glycogen (like animals, not starch like plants)
• Nigerian context: Spoilage of bread, fruits, and stored foods
• Nigerian context: Yeast in local brewing (burukutu, kunun-zaki); Penicillium for antibiotics
• Nigerian context: Edible wild mushrooms in forests; fungal diseases of crops
• Nigerian context: Aspergillus flavus produces aflatoxin in stored grains (maize, groundnuts)
• Food: Edible mushrooms, yeast in bread making and brewing
• Plant Diseases: Rusts, smuts, mildews causing crop losses
• Human Diseases: Ringworm, athlete's foot, candidiasis
• Food Spoilage: Mold growth on bread, fruits, grains
• Toxin Production: Aflatoxin contamination in stored grains
• Non-motile (cannot move from place to place)
Plants are classified into lower and higher plants:
A. Lower Plants (Non-vascular and Simple Vascular Plants):
• No true roots, stems, or leaves
• Nigerian context: Mosses on rocks and tree trunks in humid forests
• Nigerian context: Ferns in rainforest understories
• Nigerian context: Limited natural occurrence; some introduced for forestry
• Most diverse and widespread plant group
- Flower parts in multiples of three
- Examples: Maize, rice, yam, palm tree, grasses
- Nigerian context: Major food crops (maize, rice, yam)
- Flower parts in multiples of four or five
- Examples: Beans, cassava, mango, orange, cotton
- Nigerian context: Major crops (cassava, beans, cotton, cocoa)
Differences Between Lower and Higher Plants:
• No true roots, stems, leaves (in bryophytes)
• Do not require water for fertilization
• Staple crops: Cassava, yam, maize, rice
• Nigerian context: Agriculture is the backbone of Nigerian economy
• Cash crops: Cocoa, rubber, oil palm, cotton
• Medicine: Herbal medicines from various plants
• Motile at some life stage (can move)
• Nervous and muscular tissues in most
Animals are classified into two major groups:
• Nigerian context: Marine sponges in coastal waters
• Nigerian context: Jellyfish in Atlantic waters
• Nigerian context: Tapeworms in livestock and humans
• Nigerian context: Soil nematodes affecting crops; parasitic nematodes
• Nigerian context: Earthworms improve soil fertility
• Nigerian context: Giant African snail (food and pest); oysters in coastal communities
• Nigerian context: Insects as pollinators, pests, food; crustaceans in fisheries
• Nigerian context: Major source of protein; aquaculture
• Live both in water and on land
• Scaly skin, lay eggs on land
• Nigerian context: Poultry farming; wild birds
• Nigerian context: Livestock (cattle, goats, sheep); wildlife
• Teacher guides students to uproot farm plants, collect lemon, Pistia (water lettuce), break branches of shrubs
• Students observe all specimens, record observations, and make inferences
• Discussion: What characteristics distinguish plants from animals?
• Students collect local organisms and classify them into kingdoms
• Group discussion: Why is classification important in biology?
• 'All plants are green': Some plants lack chlorophyll (parasitic plants)
• 'All animals can move': Some animals are sessile (sponges, corals)
• 'Fungi are plants': Fungi are a separate kingdom with distinct characteristics
• 'All invertebrates are small': Some invertebrates can be large (octopus, squid)
• Nigeria has rich biodiversity in plants and animals across ecological zones
• Agriculture (plants and animals) employs majority of Nigerians
• Wildlife conservation important for tourism and ecosystem health
• Understanding classification helps in agriculture, conservation, and resource management
• Kingdom Fungi includes diverse heterotrophic organisms with important ecological and economic roles
• Kingdom Plantae includes autotrophic organisms essential for life on Earth
• Kingdom Animalia includes diverse heterotrophic organisms with complex organization
• All three kingdoms are essential for ecosystem functioning and human survival
• Understanding classification helps in agriculture, conservation, and resource management in Nigeria

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Kingdom Fungi Plantae Animalia classification biology SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: A characteristic feature of Kingdom Fungi is:
opts: Heterotrophic by absorption with chitin cell walls
quiz.explanation: Fungi are heterotrophic organisms that obtain nutrients by absorption and have cell walls made of chitin.

quiz.q: An example of a beneficial fungus used in Nigerian food production is:
quiz.explanation: Saccharomyces (yeast) is used in bread making and traditional brewing (burukutu, kunun-zaki) in Nigeria.

quiz.q: A characteristic feature of Kingdom Plantae is:
quiz.explanation: Plants are autotrophic organisms that make their own food through photosynthesis using chlorophyll.

quiz.q: The main difference between monocots and dicots is:
opts: Number of cotyledons and leaf venation patterns
quiz.explanation: Monocots have one cotyledon and parallel leaf veins; dicots have two cotyledons and net-like veins.

quiz.q: An example of a monocotyledonous plant common in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Maize is a monocot with one cotyledon, parallel leaf venation, and fibrous root system.

quiz.q: Animals are classified as heterotrophic because they:
opts: Depend on other organisms for food
quiz.explanation: Animals are heterotrophic organisms that cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms for nutrition.

quiz.q: A major difference between plants and animals is that plants:
opts: Have cell walls made of cellulose
quiz.explanation: Plants have cell walls made of cellulose, while animals lack cell walls entirely.

quiz.q: An example of an invertebrate commonly found in Nigerian farms is:
quiz.explanation: Earthworms are invertebrates (annelids) that improve soil fertility in Nigerian farms.

quiz.q: A vertebrate commonly reared for food in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Catfish are vertebrates (fish) commonly farmed and consumed in Nigeria as a source of protein.

quiz.q: Understanding plant and animal classification is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Helps in crop improvement, pest control, and livestock management
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of classification helps farmers identify crops, pests, beneficial organisms, and manage livestock effectively.
